title: Navarin d'Agneau
subtitle: French spring lamb stew with new vegetables
description: Navarin d'agneau printanier is the classic French spring lamb stew: neck or shoulder pieces browned in fat, braised in stock and white wine with tomatoes and garlic, then finished with new potatoes, young carrots, peas, and turnips added in stages according to their cooking time. The result is a stew that is simultaneously robust and fresh — heavier than a ragout but lighter than a bourguignon. It is the dish that announces spring in French home cooking.
sourceNotes: Navarin d'agneau printanier is a canonical dish of French spring cookery, codified by Escoffier in Le Guide Culinaire (1903). The word navarin comes from navet (turnip), a defining ingredient of the dish. Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) contains a closely related recipe.

recipeTools.notes: Oven-safe with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enamelled cast iron is ideal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French braise of lamb or mutton with vegetables, taking its name from navet (turnip). The printanier version adds spring vegetables in the final stages of cooking.
A bundle of herbs — typically bay leaf, thyme, and parsley stalks — tied together and added to braises and stocks. Removed before serving.

ingredients.notes: Or bone-in shoulder, cut into 5 cm pieces
Small — left whole or halved if large
Or regular carrots cut into batons

steps: Heat the oven to 160 °C fan. Season the lamb and dust with flour. Heat the butter and oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown the lamb in batches for 4–5 minutes per batch until deeply coloured. Remove to a plate.
Reduce to medium heat. Cook the onion for 5 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute.

Pour in the white wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the crushed tomatoes and stock. Tie the thyme and bay into a bouquet garni and add. Return the lamb, bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven.
Braise for 45 minutes. Remove from the oven.

Add the potatoes, carrots, and turnips to the casserole, pushing them down into the liquid. Return to the oven uncovered and cook for a further 30–35 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the lamb falls from the bone.
Remove the bouquet garni. Add the peas and stir gently — they need only 2–3 minutes in the hot stew. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Scatter with parsley and serve from the casserole.

content.text: is lighter than a bourguignon — the vegetables go in during the final 35 minutes rather than being cooked separately and added at the end. Add them too early and they will collapse; add them at the right stage and they absorb the lamb cooking liquor and become tender while still holding their shape.
Brown the lamb and build the braise

Heat the oven to 160 °C fan. Season {{lamb-neck-fillet}} of lamb and dust with flour. Heat butter and oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown the lamb in batches for 4–5 minutes until deeply coloured. Remove. Cook the onion in the remaining fat for 5 minutes, add the garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute, then pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine. Add {{lamb-stock}} of stock, crushed tomatoes, and a
. Return the lamb, bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven for 45 minutes.

Remove from the oven. Add {{new-potatoes}} of new potatoes, {{baby-carrot}} of baby carrots, and {{turnip}} of turnip wedges, pushing them into the liquid. Return to the oven uncovered for 30–35 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the lamb is falling from the bone.
Remove the bouquet garni. Stir in {{frozen-peas}} of peas and leave for 2–3 minutes in the hot stew. Taste and adjust seasoning, scatter with parsley, and serve from the casserole.
